audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for feeding sign, walking on sea/I am, bread from heaven discourse, flesh/blood hard saying, Spirit/life, Peter confession.

verses.restored: After these things Jesus went away to the other side of the sea of Galilee, the sea of Tiberias.
A great crowd followed him, because they were beholding the signs he was doing on those who were weak.
Jesus went up into the mountain, and there he was sitting with his disciples.
Now the Passover, the feast of the Judeans, was near.
Lifting up his eyes and beholding that a great crowd was coming to him, Jesus says to Philip, From where shall we buy bread so these may eat?
He said this testing him, for he himself knew what he was about to do.
Philip answered him, Two hundred denarii of bread is not enough for them, so that each may receive a little.
One of his disciples, Andrew, Simon Peter's brother, says to him,
There is a little boy here who has five barley loaves and two fish; but what are these for so many?
Jesus said, Make the people recline. There was much grass in the place. So the men reclined, about five thousand in number.
Jesus therefore took the loaves, and giving thanks, distributed to those reclining; likewise also from the fish, as much as they wanted.
When they were filled, he says to his disciples, Gather the remaining broken pieces, so that nothing may be lost.
So they gathered and filled twelve baskets of broken pieces from the five barley loaves, which remained over to those who had eaten.
Therefore the people, seeing the sign he did, were saying, This is truly the Prophet coming into the world.
Jesus, knowing that they were about to come and seize him so they might make him king, withdrew again to the mountain by himself alone.
When evening came, his disciples went down to the sea.
Entering a boat, they were going across the sea to Capernaum. Darkness had already come, and Jesus had not yet come to them.
The sea was being stirred because a strong wind was blowing.
Having rowed about twenty-five or thirty stadia, they behold Jesus walking on the sea and coming near the boat, and they were afraid.
But he says to them, I am; do not fear.
Therefore they were willing to receive him into the boat, and immediately the boat came to the land to which they were going.

The next day the crowd standing on the other side of the sea saw that there had been no other little boat there except one, and that Jesus had not entered the boat with his disciples, but his disciples went away alone.
Other boats came from Tiberias near the place where they ate the bread after the Lord gave thanks.
When the crowd saw that Jesus was not there, nor his disciples, they entered the boats and went to Capernaum seeking Jesus.
Finding him across the sea, they said to him, Rabbi, when did you come here?
Jesus answered them, Amen, amen, I say to you: you seek me not because you saw signs, but because you ate from the loaves and were filled.
Do not work for the food that perishes, but for the food remaining into age-long life, which the Son of Humanity will give you; for on this one the Father, the Divine, has set his seal.
They said to him, What shall we do, so that we may work the works of the Divine?
Jesus answered and said to them, This is the work of the Divine: that you trust into the one whom that One sent.
They said to him, What sign then do you do, so that we may see and trust you? What do you work?
Our fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, as it has been written: He gave them bread from heaven to eat.
Jesus said to them, Amen, amen, I say to you: Moses has not given you the bread from heaven, but my Father gives you the true bread from heaven.
For the bread of the Divine is the one coming down from heaven and giving life to the world.
They said to him, Lord, always give us this bread.
Jesus said to them, I am the bread of life. The one coming to me will certainly not hunger, and the one trusting into me will certainly never thirst.
But I said to you that you have seen me and yet do not trust.
All that the Father gives me will come to me, and the one coming to me I will certainly not cast out,
because I have come down from heaven not to do my own will but the will of the One who sent me.
This is the will of the One who sent me: that of all he has given me I should lose nothing, but raise it up on the last day.
For this is the will of my Father: that everyone beholding the Son and trusting into him may have age-long life, and I will raise that one up on the last day.
Therefore the Judeans were grumbling about him because he said, I am the bread that came down from heaven.
They were saying, Is this not Jesus son of Joseph, whose father and mother we know? How now does he say, I have come down from heaven?
Jesus answered and said to them, Do not grumble with one another.
No one is able to come to me unless the Father who sent me draws that one, and I will raise that one up on the last day.
It has been written in the Prophets: And they will all be taught by the Divine. Everyone who hears from the Father and learns comes to me.
Not that anyone has seen the Father except the one being from the Divine; this one has seen the Father.
Amen, amen, I say to you: the one trusting has age-long life.
I am the bread of life.
Your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died.
This is the bread coming down from heaven, so that one may eat from it and not die.
I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats from this bread, that one will live into the age; and the bread that I will give for the life of the world is my embodied life.
Therefore the Judeans were disputing with one another, saying, How is this one able to give us his embodied life to eat?
Jesus therefore said to them, Amen, amen, I say to you: unless you eat the embodied life of the Son of Humanity and drink his blood, you do not have life in yourselves.
The one eating my embodied life and drinking my blood has age-long life, and I will raise that one up on the last day.
For my embodied life is true food, and my blood is true drink.
The one eating my embodied life and drinking my blood remains in me, and I in that one.
Just as the living Father sent me, and I live because of the Father, so also the one eating me will live because of me.
This is the bread that came down from heaven, not as the fathers ate and died; the one eating this bread will live into the age.
He said these things teaching in a synagogue in Capernaum.
Therefore many of his disciples, hearing, said, This word is hard; who is able to hear it?
Jesus, knowing in himself that his disciples were grumbling about this, said to them, Does this cause you to stumble?
Then what if you behold the Son of Humanity ascending where he was before?
The Spirit is the one making alive; ordinary human nature profits nothing. The words I have spoken to you are Spirit and are life.
But there are some among you who do not trust. For Jesus knew from the beginning who those were who did not trust, and who was the one handing him over.
He was saying, Because of this I have said to you that no one is able to come to me unless it has been given to that one from the Father.
From this, many of his disciples went away to the things behind and no longer walked with him.
Therefore Jesus said to the twelve, Do you also want to go away?
Simon Peter answered him, Lord, to whom shall we go? You have words of age-long life.
And we have trusted and have known that you are the Holy One of the Divine.
Jesus answered them, Did I not choose you, the twelve? Yet one of you is adversarial.
He was speaking of Judas son of Simon Iscariot, for this one, one of the twelve, was about to hand him over.
